runtimeDecision.choice: Option A: TypeScript-only monorepo (Next.js fullstack + Bun workers)
runtimeDecision.reasoning: LiteLLM has an official TypeScript-compatible proxy mode and the 'litellm' npm package covers all required providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepSeek) with unified streaming and tool-call normalization. LangGraph is Python-native but its core concepts (state machine, node delegation, interrupt/resume checkpoints) are fully reproducible in TypeScript without the overhead of a second runtime — the community 'langgraph.js' (now @langchain/langgraph) is production-ready. The Vercel AI SDK gives first-class streaming, tool-call parsing, and multi-provider support in TypeScript natively. Running Python FastAPI alongside Next.js (Option B/C) doubles infra complexity: separate Docker images, separate health checks, cross-service auth, different logging pipelines, two package managers, two CI chains. For a long-running agent executor, Bun workers (or Node.js worker_threads) provide genuine OS threads without V8 GIL constraints. The monorepo stays in one language, one type system (shared Zod schemas end-to-end), one deployment unit, and one developer mental model. Dev-experience trade-off: Python has a richer ML/agent ecosystem (LangGraph native, smolagents, CrewAI) but for a workflow organizer that calls LLMs via API — not trains models — those libraries add more ceremony than value; direct SDK calls + a thin orchestrator is simpler. Decision: TypeScript monorepo with Bun as the worker runtime, Next.js App Router as the API/BFF layer, PostgreSQL + Redis for state/queues, BullMQ for job scheduling.

executionEngine.workflowParsing: Workflow definition JSON is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) stored in PostgreSQL as JSONB. Schema: nodes[] each typed as 'agent' | 'human_gate' | 'condition' | 'parallel_fork' | 'parallel_join'. Each agent node carries {agentId, inputMapping: JSONata expr, outputMapping: JSONata expr}. On run start, the engine resolves the full adjacency list, topologically sorts independent branches, and builds a RunPlan: {entryNodes: string[], nodeMap: Map<nodeId, ResolvedNode>, edgeMap: Map<nodeId, string[]>}. No static compilation — the plan is built at runtime and serialized into the run record so a resumed run re-uses the same plan without re-resolving.
executionEngine.orchestratorInstantiation: The orchestrator is a special agent node defined in the workflow with role='orchestrator'. It is given a system prompt that includes: (1) the full workflow definition as structured JSON, (2) the names and descriptions of all sub-agent nodes, (3) a tool schema for each sub-agent with the signature: {name: 'invoke_agent', parameters: {nodeId: string, input: object}}. The orchestrator is instantiated via the Vercel AI SDK streamText() call with toolChoice='auto' and a tools map built dynamically from the resolved workflow plan. The orchestrator LLM decides execution order for dynamic branches; static branches (linear or fork/join) are handled by the engine directly without an orchestrator.
executionEngine.agentDelegation: Sub-agents are invoked via tool calls from the orchestrator. When the orchestrator emits a tool call {invoke_agent, {nodeId, input}}, the execution engine intercepts it in the onToolCall handler of streamText(), enqueues a BullMQ child job for that node, and registers a Promise that resolves when the child job completes. The parent orchestrator job is suspended (job.moveToDelayed or a polling approach with MQ) until the child Promise resolves. For parallel fork nodes, all child promises are awaited with Promise.all(). Each sub-agent runs in its own Bun worker thread, receives the resolved input (after JSONata mapping), calls the LLM, and emits its output back via postMessage to the parent. Sub-agents do NOT call other agents directly — all delegation flows through the orchestrator tool-call mechanism, preserving a single coordination point and a clean audit trail.
executionEngine.streaming: Each Bun worker streams LLM chunks back to the main thread via postMessage({type:'chunk', seq, content}). The main thread writes log events to a Redis Stream (XADD runs:{runId}:logs) with fields {seq, ts, level, nodeId, agentId, chunk}. The SSE endpoint (/api/runs/:runId/logs) subscribes to the Redis Stream using XREAD BLOCK 0 COUNT 50 STREAMS runs:{runId}:logs $, reads new entries in a loop, and writes them as SSE events to the HTTP response. Backpressure is handled via Node.js writable stream drain events. Clients can reconnect with since={lastSeq} and the SSE handler replays from that offset using XRANGE.
executionEngine.errorHandling: Three-tier error handling. Tier 1 — transient LLM errors (rate limit 429, timeout, 503): caught in the provider adapter, retried with exponential backoff using the agent's retryPolicy (default: 3 attempts, 2s/4s/8s). Tier 2 — agent-level failure (max retries exhausted, context overflow, tool parse error): the node is marked status='failed', the error is written to the log stream, and the engine evaluates whether the workflow has a fallback edge from that node. If a fallback edge exists, execution continues on the fallback path; if not, the run transitions to 'failed'. Tier 3 — orchestrator failure: the entire run is checkpointed with status='suspended' and a human operator can inspect and resume. BullMQ job failure triggers the job's failedReason to be written into the run record in Postgres via the job's failed event handler.

liteLLMIntegration.keyManagement: API keys are stored encrypted at rest in PostgreSQL using AES-256-GCM. The column type is bytea. The encryption key is a 256-bit secret stored in an environment variable PROVIDER_KEY_ENCRYPTION_SECRET (loaded from a secrets manager like Doppler or Infisecret in production). On read, the key is decrypted in-process just before the LLM call and injected into the provider client constructor — it is never logged, never sent to Redis, never serialized into job payloads. The key hint (last 4 chars) is stored in a separate plaintext column for display. Key rotation is handled by re-encrypting with a new encryption key without changing the provider key itself.
liteLLMIntegration.toolNormalization: A ToolNormalizer class accepts the internal tool definition format {name, description, parameters: JSONSchema} and emits provider-specific formats. For Anthropic: tools array with input_schema. For OpenAI/DeepSeek: tools array with function.parameters. For Gemini: functionDeclarations with parameters. Tool CALL normalization (parsing the LLM response) maps each provider's response format back to a canonical ToolCall {name, arguments: object}. Anthropic returns content blocks with type='tool_use'; OpenAI returns choices[0].message.tool_calls; Gemini returns candidates[0].content.parts with functionCall. The normalizer handles all three formats in a switch block, reducing orchestrator code to a single canonical interface.
liteLLMIntegration.costTracking: Every LLM call goes through a CostTracker middleware. After each streaming completion, the provider returns usage: {promptTokens, completionTokens} in the final chunk (all four providers support this). The tracker looks up the model's inputCostPer1k and outputCostPer1k from the models table, computes cost = (promptTokens/1000 * inputCostPer1k) + (completionTokens/1000 * outputCostPer1k), and writes a cost_events row {runId, nodeId, agentId, modelId, inputTokens, completionTokens, costUsd, ts} to Postgres. This is fire-and-forget (no await on the hot path). Aggregate cost is queryable via the /api/cost/runs/:runId endpoint with a simple GROUP BY.
liteLLMIntegration.rateLimiting: A sliding-window rate limiter is implemented in Redis using a sorted set per provider+model key: ratelimit:{providerId}:{modelId}. Each request adds a member with score=Date.now() and value=requestId, then removes members older than the window (e.g. 60 000ms for per-minute limits), then counts members. If count >= limit, the request is rejected with a 429-equivalent error and the caller backs off. Limits are stored in the models table (rpmLimit, tpmLimit). For token-per-minute limits, the score stores token count and the sum is computed with ZRANGEBYSCORE. This is a single-service lock-free approach; for multi-instance deployments, Lua scripts ensure atomicity.
liteLLMIntegration.fallbackChain: The FallbackChain is configured per-agent as an ordered array of {modelId, maxAttempts} entries. On primary model failure (rate limit, context overflow, or max retries), the engine advances to the next entry in the chain. Each chain entry can reference a different provider. Example chain for an agent: [{modelId:'claude-sonnet-4-6', maxAttempts:3}, {modelId:'gpt-4o', maxAttempts:2}, {modelId:'gemini-2-5-pro', maxAttempts:1}]. The chain is tried sequentially, not in parallel. If the entire chain is exhausted, the node fails. Fallback events are logged as level='warn' in the log stream with {nodeId, fromModel, toModel, reason}.

queueDesign.reasoning: BullMQ is chosen over Inngest because: (1) it runs fully on-prem with no external SaaS dependency, which matters for API key security — job payloads never leave the server; (2) it supports delayed jobs, job dependencies (parent/child), and rate limiting natively; (3) it has first-class TypeScript types; (4) it supports priority queues needed for human-gate urgent signals. Inngest is excellent for serverless but the requirement explicitly excludes serverless. A custom queue is rejected because Redis Streams or pg-boss would need significant engineering to match BullMQ's feature set.
jobTypes: workflow.start — payload: {runId, workflowId, workflowVersion, input}; starts the orchestrator and top-level node fan-out
node.execute — payload: {runId, nodeId, agentId, resolvedInput, parentJobId}; executes a single agent node in a Bun worker
node.human_gate — payload: {runId, nodeId, promptMessage}; transitions node to 'awaiting_human', creates a delayed job that auto-rejects after timeout
run.checkpoint — payload: {runId, checkpointSeq, state: CheckpointState}; writes checkpoint to Postgres — triggered every N node completions and on any node failure
cost.record — payload: {runId, nodeId, agentId, modelId, inputTokens, outputTokens, costUsd}; fire-and-forget cost persistence
run.cancel — payload: {runId}; sets a cancellation flag in Redis, gracefully drains active node jobs for the run
queueDesign.concurrencyModel: Three worker pools with separate BullMQ workers: (1) orchestrator-pool: concurrency=5 — orchestrator jobs are long-lived and IO-bound, 5 simultaneous workflows; (2) node-pool: concurrency=20 — individual agent node executions, each in a Bun worker_thread; (3) system-pool: concurrency=50 — checkpoints, cost records, fast bookkeeping jobs. The node-pool worker spawns a Bun worker thread per job (not per-worker, to isolate LLM call memory), capped at MAX_CONCURRENT_AGENTS=20 env var. For a single workflow with parallel branches, all parallel nodes compete for node-pool slots — the first 20 run immediately, rest queue behind them.

backend.crashRecovery: {"strategy": "Checkpoint-based resume with Postgres as the source of truth. Every workflow run has a checkpoints table row with columns: {runId, seq, nodeStates: JSONB, pendingNodeIds: text[], completedNodeIds: text[], orchestratorMessageHistory: JSONB, createdAt}. Checkpoints are written: (1) after every agent node completes successfully, (2) after every orchestrator tool call resolves, (3) every 60 seconds for long-running nodes via a heartbeat job. On server restart or BullMQ worker crash, a startup reconciliation job (runs on app boot) queries runs WHERE status IN ('running', 'queued') AND updatedAt < NOW() - INTERVAL '2 minutes'. For each stale run, the latest checkpoint is loaded, orphaned BullMQ jobs are cleaned (bull.clean(0, 'active')), and a recovery job is enqueued that reconstructs the RunPlan from the checkpoint, re-enqueues incomplete node jobs with their checkpointed inputs, and restores the orchestrator's message history so it has full context of what was already completed. The orchestrator resumes with a synthetic user message injecting the completed node results, then continues from where it left off. Human-gate nodes that were awaiting input before crash are re-signaled to 'awaiting_human' status and the frontend is notified via SSE reconnect. Idempotency: each node.execute job carries an idempotencyKey = hash(runId+nodeId+retryCount), preventing duplicate LLM calls if a job is accidentally re-queued."}
criticalDetails: Shared Zod schemas in packages/shared/src/schemas/ enforce type safety across the Next.js API layer and Bun worker layer — no runtime type mismatches between job payload producer and consumer.
The SSE endpoint uses Node.js response streaming with res.setHeader('Cache-Control','no-cache') and res.setHeader('X-Accel-Buffering','no') — critical for Nginx proxies which buffer SSE by default.

WebSockets are NOT needed. SSE is unidirectional server-to-client (sufficient for log streaming). Human-gate signals go client-to-server via the POST /signal endpoint (standard REST). The latency difference between SSE and WebSocket is irrelevant at log-streaming granularity.

All LLM calls must set a per-call timeout (default 120s for Claude, 90s for OpenAI) via AbortController — without this, a hung provider connection blocks the Bun worker thread indefinitely, exhausting the node-pool.

The orchestrator's message history is stored in the checkpoint as the raw messages[] array (system+user+assistant+tool_result turns). On resume, the history is replayed into the LLM context verbatim — no summarization on the hot path. For very long runs (>100k tokens of history), a background compaction job summarizes older turns and replaces them with a summary message, triggered when history exceeds 80% of the model's context window.

PostgreSQL row-level locking (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED) is used on the runs table during checkpoint writes to prevent concurrent worker instances from corrupting state when running multiple server instances.

API keys in job payloads are NEVER included — workers query the database at job execution time to retrieve and decrypt the key for the specific provider, ensuring keys are not persisted in the Redis queue.

The workflow definition JSON supports a 'humanGateTimeout' field per human_gate node (default: 24h). After timeout, the node auto-transitions to 'timed_out' and the configured fallback edge (approve_by_default or reject_by_default) is followed.
Rate limit state in Redis uses TTL-based expiry on the sorted sets — no manual cleanup needed. Each ZADD sets the key TTL to the window duration, ensuring stale rate limit data does not accumulate.

The monorepo structure: apps/web (Next.js App Router, API routes + frontend), apps/worker (Bun, BullMQ worker processes), packages/shared (Zod schemas, types), packages/db (Drizzle ORM, migrations), packages/llm (provider adapters, ToolNormalizer, CostTracker, FallbackChain). Turborepo manages the build pipeline with remote caching.

frameworkDecision.choice: Vite + React SPA + separate API (Option B)
frameworkDecision.reasoning: The Agent Workflow Organizer is a canvas-heavy, realtime-streaming, client-centric app — not a content or SEO-driven site. Option B is the clear winner for three structural reasons. First, SSE/streaming: Next.js App Router RSC runs on the server edge; SSE EventSource connections must be opened on the client and kept alive across navigation — RSC re-renders on route changes interfere with persistent SSE connections, and the Next.js streaming model (React Suspense + fetch streaming) is entirely different from EventSource-based agent token streaming. A pure SPA holds one long-lived EventSource per run, no server teardown. Second, canvas state: ReactFlow's entire model (nodes, edges, viewport, selection) must live in client memory with zero server round-trips. RSC forces a hard mental split between server and client components; every ReactFlow node, custom hook, and zustand store must be explicitly marked 'use client', adding friction with zero benefit. A Vite SPA is natively all-client — no split, no accidental serialization bugs, no hydration mismatches on complex canvas state. Third, routing complexity is low: the app has ~8 routes total, no SEO requirements, no public pages needing pre-rendering. Next.js App Router's power (nested layouts with co-located data fetching, RSC streaming) adds zero value but significant complexity (server/client boundary bugs, edge runtime limitations for WebSocket/SSE, opaque build output). Remix shares the same SSR overhead concern. Vite + React SPA + a dedicated FastAPI or Node/Hono backend delivers: sub-100ms HMR, tree-shakable bundle, full canvas and SSE freedom, clean API boundary for multi-provider LLM proxying, and a separate backend that can scale independently when streaming token throughput grows.
tradeoffs: No built-in SSR means initial HTML is a blank shell — acceptable because the app requires auth before any meaningful content renders, so SSR SEO provides zero value
API must be deployed and CORS-configured separately — manageable with a single Hono/FastAPI service and a shared Docker Compose or Railway config
Code splitting must be done manually with React.lazy — mitigated by route-level lazy loading which Vite's rollup config handles cleanly
No file-system routing — with only 8 routes, TanStack Router's typed file-based routing (or React Router v7 loaders) provides explicit, type-safe routes without the Next.js complexity overhead
Auth must be implemented client-side — use a lightweight session cookie or JWT stored in httpOnly cookie via the backend; a dedicated /api/auth route handles OAuth flows

stores: providerStore — API keys (encrypted at rest in localStorage via subtle crypto), provider health status, rate-limit counters per provider. Never holds raw keys in memory longer than needed for a single API call. Shape: { providers: Record<ProviderId, ProviderConfig>, keyStatus: Record<ProviderId, 'valid'|'invalid'|'unchecked'> }

agentStore — all named agents with their id, name, modelId, providerId, systemPrompt, temperature, maxTokens. Persisted to IndexedDB via zustand-persist. Shape: { agents: Record<AgentId, AgentConfig>, selectedAgentId: string|null }

workflowStore — the list of saved workflows (metadata only: id, name, createdAt, updatedAt, nodeCount). Full workflow JSON is lazy-loaded per workflow. Shape: { workflows: WorkflowMeta[], activeWorkflowId: string|null }

uiStore — global UI state: sidebar open/closed, active panel tab, theme, modal stack (array of open modal ids with their props), notification toasts queue. Shape: { sidebarOpen: boolean, activeTab: TabId, modals: ModalEntry[], toasts: Toast[] }

runStore — execution state: current run id, status, per-node status map, live token counts, cost accumulator, SSE connection state. Deliberately separate from canvasStore so SSE updates do NOT cause ReactFlow to re-render. Shape: { runId: string|null, status: RunStatus, nodeStatuses: Record<NodeId, NodeRunStatus>, streamingNodeId: string|null, tokenBuffer: string, costAccumulator: CostBreakdown, sseState: 'connecting'|'open'|'closed'|'error' }

canvasState.approach: ReactFlow's built-in useNodesState + useEdgesState hooks manage the authoritative nodes/edges arrays inside the WorkflowCanvas component. These are NOT in a global zustand store. They are lifted to a CanvasContext (React context) scoped to the canvas route. The canvas serializes to a WorkflowJSON shape when the user saves — at that point the serialized JSON is sent to the workflowStore and persisted. This isolation means SSE-driven runStore updates never touch the canvas node/edge arrays; instead, node visual state during runs is communicated via a separate nodeRunStatuses Map stored in runStore, and each custom node reads ONLY its own entry from this map using a selector (useRunNodeStatus(id)) that is memoized with useShallowEqual. This prevents all other nodes from re-rendering when one node's streaming status changes.
canvasState.undoRedo: Implemented with a custom useHistory hook wrapping the canvas context. The hook maintains a stack of WorkflowSnapshot objects (serialized nodes + edges, max depth 50). It intercepts onNodesChange and onEdgesChange events from ReactFlow, debounces them at 300ms (to batch drag-move events into one undo step), and pushes snapshots. Keyboard shortcuts Cmd+Z / Cmd+Shift+Z dispatch to the history hook. The hook exposes { canUndo, canRedo, undo, redo, historyLength }. Undo/redo is DISABLED during an active run to prevent canvas mutations that would desync from the backend execution graph.

runExecutionState.approach: runStore (zustand) holds all execution state. It is the single source of truth for the live run overlay. Components subscribe with fine-grained selectors: e.g. useRunStore(s => s.nodeStatuses[nodeId]) inside each custom node. The store exposes actions: startRun(workflowId), handleSseEvent(event), abortRun(), resolveHumanGate(nodeId, decision). The SSE manager (a singleton class, NOT a React component) calls handleSseEvent directly on the store, bypassing React entirely for the hot path. React only schedules re-renders when the zustand subscriber diffs detect a change.
runExecutionState.streamingStrategy: Token streaming uses a double-buffer approach to prevent per-character re-renders. The SSE manager accumulates tokens into a rawBuffer string. A 16ms requestAnimationFrame loop (one per active run) flushes rawBuffer into runStore.tokenBuffer. The active agent node's TokenStreamDisplay component subscribes only to tokenBuffer and uses CSS animation (opacity pulse) rather than DOM string appends. When a chunk boundary is detected (agent finishes), tokenBuffer is committed to runStore.nodeStatuses[id].fullOutput and tokenBuffer is cleared. This caps canvas re-renders to 60fps max regardless of token arrival rate.

stateManagement.interactions: The four stores interact in a strictly unidirectional flow with no circular dependencies. providerStore and agentStore are read by the workflow editor panels but never written by the canvas. workflowStore is written when the user saves; the canvas reads workflow JSON on mount to initialize CanvasContext. runStore is written only by the SSE manager and user actions (start/abort/human-gate). The canvas reads runStore for visual overlays but never writes to it. This means: editing a workflow never touches runStore, a live run never mutates workflowStore, and provider key changes never cascade into the canvas.

canvasNodeTypes.name: AgentNode
canvasNodeTypes.description: Core execution node. Renders the agent's name, model badge (e.g. 'claude-sonnet-4-6'), and provider icon. During a run it shows a streaming token display (scrollable, monospace, max 10 lines visible with expand), a status ring (idle/queued/running/done/error) as an animated SVG border, and token count + estimated cost in the footer. Has one default input handle and one default output handle. Clicking opens an AgentNodeSidebar that lets the user swap the agent binding without leaving the canvas.

canvasNodeTypes.name: ConditionNode
canvasNodeTypes.description: Branching node with one input and two output handles labeled 'true' and 'false'. The condition is a JS expression evaluated against the previous node's output JSON (e.g. 'output.sentiment === positive'). Rendered as a diamond shape via CSS clip-path. During a run, the taken branch edge is highlighted and the untaken branch is dimmed.

canvasNodeTypes.name: FanOutNode
canvasNodeTypes.description: Splits a single input into N parallel branches. The number of output handles is configurable (2–8). Each branch can have an optional label. Rendered as a horizontal bar with emanating lines. During a run, output edges animate simultaneously with staggered particle delay to convey true parallelism.

canvasNodeTypes.name: AggregatorNode
canvasNodeTypes.description: Collects results from N parallel branches into one output. Aggregation strategy is configurable: 'concat', 'json_array', 'best_of_n' (uses a secondary agent to pick), 'custom_js'. Shows a progress bar during a run indicating how many branches have completed. Blocked visually (greyed border) until all inputs arrive.

canvasNodeTypes.name: LoopNode
canvasNodeTypes.description: Wraps a subgraph in a loop. Has a loop-back edge (rendered as a curved arrow returning to the node's own input handle). Loop condition is evaluated after each iteration (max-iterations hard cap to prevent infinite loops). Shows current iteration count during run as a badge.

canvasNodeTypes.name: HumanGateNode
canvasNodeTypes.description: Pause point requiring human approval. Rendered with a lock icon and amber color. During a run, triggers the HumanGateModal which overlays the entire canvas and blocks further execution. The node pulses with an amber ring animation while awaiting input. Stores the approval/rejection decision and the reviewer's optional note in the run trace.

canvasNodeTypes.name: InputNode
canvasNodeTypes.description: Entry point for the workflow. Accepts the initial user payload as JSON or plain text. Has no input handle, only an output handle. On the canvas it shows the input schema (defined as a JSON schema snippet). When starting a run, a modal prompts the user to fill in input values matching the schema.

canvasNodeTypes.name: OutputNode
canvasNodeTypes.description: Terminal node that captures the final workflow output. Has one input handle and no output. During a run it shows the final result inline with syntax highlighting (JSON or markdown). After completion the result can be copied or downloaded. Multiple OutputNodes are allowed for workflows with parallel terminal branches.

canvasNodeTypes.name: ToolNode
canvasNodeTypes.description: Calls an external tool or API (web search, code execution, HTTP endpoint, file read/write). Configured with tool type, endpoint URL, auth headers (stored in providerStore, referenced by id — never stored in the workflow JSON directly), and a JSON schema for input/output mapping. Renders with a wrench icon and the tool name. During a run shows the HTTP status and response time.

realtimeUIDesign.sseStrategy: A singleton SseManager class (not a React component or hook) owns the EventSource lifecycle. It is instantiated once when a run starts via runStore.startRun(). It opens EventSource(api/runs/{runId}/stream, { withCredentials: true }). On message, it parses the event type field and dispatches to runStore.handleSseEvent(event) synchronously — this is a plain function call, not a React setState. Reconnection: onError triggers an exponential backoff reconnect (500ms, 1s, 2s, 4s, cap 30s) using a class-level reconnectTimer. Before reconnecting, it sends GET /api/runs/{runId}/state to resync node statuses (the backend maintains durable run state so no events are lost on reconnect). On AbortRun or run completion (event type 'run.complete'), the SseManager calls close() and nulls the EventSource reference. The SseManager is exposed on the window object in dev mode for debugging. SSE event schema: { type: 'node.started'|'node.token'|'node.completed'|'node.error'|'run.complete'|'human_gate.pending'|'cost.update', nodeId: string, payload: object, seqNo: number }. seqNo enables gap detection — if seqNo jumps, a full state resync is triggered.
realtimeUIDesign.humanGateFlow: When SSE delivers event type 'human_gate.pending' with nodeId, runStore sets nodeStatuses[nodeId].status = 'awaiting_human' and sets runStore.activeHumanGate = { nodeId, promptText, approveLabel, rejectLabel }. The HumanGateOverlay component (rendered at the root layout level, not inside the canvas) watches runStore.activeHumanGate with useEffect. When it becomes non-null, it triggers a full-screen modal with an opaque backdrop (z-index above canvas, below nav). The modal shows the agent's last message, the prompt text, and two buttons. Keyboard: Enter = approve, Escape = reject (configurable). On decision, the component calls runStore.resolveHumanGate(nodeId, 'approved'|'rejected', note) which POSTs to POST /api/runs/{runId}/gates/{nodeId}/decision and clears activeHumanGate. The HumanGateNode on the canvas updates its visual state via the normal nodeStatuses subscription. The modal cannot be dismissed without making a decision — the close button is absent. An optional timer can be configured on the node; if it expires without decision the gate auto-rejects and a warning toast appears.
realtimeUIDesign.animationApproach: All canvas animations use CSS custom properties and keyframes, NOT JS-driven transforms, to keep the main thread free for SSE parsing and React reconciliation. Edge animations during run: each custom EdgeType reads runStore to determine if it is 'active' (source node running, target node queued). Active edges receive a CSS class that triggers a SVG stroke-dashoffset animation — a moving dashed line that appears as flowing particles without any JS animation loop. The dasharray and dashoffset are set as CSS variables on the SVG path element. Node status ring: each AgentNode has a <circle> SVG element for the status ring. Its stroke color and animation-name are driven by a statusClass computed from nodeRunStatus. States: idle (no animation, grey), queued (slow pulse, blue), running (fast pulse + rotation, indigo), done (solid green, 2s scale-in then steady), error (red flash 3x then steady red). Token streaming text: uses CSS animation 'typing-cursor' (blinking block cursor appended to tokenBuffer). The token text div uses font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums to prevent layout shift as numbers update. Cost counter in the run panel uses CSS counter-style animation (countUp keyframes on the numeric portion) for a satisfying live-update feel without JS animation libraries.

routes.name: Landing / Auth Gate
routes.purpose: Root route. If the user has a valid session cookie, redirects immediately to /workflows. If not, renders a minimal centered login card (email magic link or OAuth via the backend). No canvas, no heavy imports. This route is lazy-loaded last since authenticated users never see it after onboarding.

routes.name: Onboarding
routes.purpose: First-time setup wizard (3 steps): (1) Connect at least one LLM provider API key, (2) Create first agent, (3) Open or create first workflow. Uses a stepped layout with progress indicator. On completion sets onboardingComplete flag in localStorage and redirects to /workflows. Can be re-entered via Settings.

routes.name: Workflow List
routes.purpose: Dashboard showing all saved workflows as cards in a masonry grid. Each card shows name, node count, last modified date, last run status badge (success/failed/never), and a thumbnail SVG snapshot of the canvas layout. Actions: New Workflow, Duplicate, Delete, Rename (inline). Supports search and sort (name, date, run status).

routes.name: Workflow Canvas Editor
routes.purpose: The primary route. Full-screen canvas using ReactFlow. Left sidebar: node palette (drag to add), agent library list, workflow properties panel. Right sidebar (contextual): opens when a node is selected to show that node's config form. Top bar: workflow name (editable inline), Save button, Run button, undo/redo buttons, zoom controls, export button. Bottom: history timeline of past runs for this workflow. Run mode: when a run is active the canvas enters run-mode overlay (nodes show live status, a run control bar appears at the bottom with Abort, Pause, run metadata).

routes.name: Run Detail / Replay
routes.purpose: Read-only view of a completed run. Shows the canvas in a frozen state with all nodes colored by their final status. Clicking a node shows its full input, output, token count, cost, and duration. Bottom panel has three tabs: Logs (full chronological event log), Data/IO (input/output for each node as JSON), Trace Timeline (horizontal Gantt-style visualization showing which nodes ran in parallel and for how long).

routes.name: Agent Library
routes.purpose: CRUD for all saved agents. Table view with columns: name, provider, model, system prompt preview, last used, created date. Click to open AgentDetailDrawer for full edit. New Agent button opens a slide-over form. Agents can be tagged and filtered. Import/export agents as JSON. Shows model availability badge (green if the provider key for this agent is valid, red if not).

routes.name: Settings
routes.purpose: Tabbed settings page. Tabs: Providers (add/remove/test API keys for Claude, GPT, Gemini, DeepSeek — keys are displayed as masked after save, stored encrypted in the backend), Models (view available models per provider with context window and cost/token info), Appearance (theme: light/dark/system, canvas grid style, node density), Account (profile, email, danger zone: delete account). Provider key testing hits POST /api/providers/{id}/test which makes a minimal API call to verify the key.

routes.name: Provider Detail
routes.purpose: Dedicated page for a single provider showing: key management, usage statistics (tokens consumed this month per model, cost breakdown), rate limit status, and model list with individual enable/disable toggles. Links back to /settings.

frontend.componentStrategy: Use shadcn/ui as the default component layer for all standard UI elements (buttons, inputs, dropdowns, dialogs, tabs, tooltips, badges, cards, tables, sliders). shadcn/ui's copy-paste model is ideal here: components live in /src/components/ui/ and can be modified per project needs without fighting a library's internal API. Radix UI primitives are the foundation shadcn/ui uses, so they are already available for cases needing lower-level control — use Radix directly when building composites that shadcn/ui does not cover (e.g., a custom ContextMenu with dynamic items based on node type, or a DragOverlay for node palette drag-and-drop). Custom-built components are required for: (1) All ReactFlow node and edge types — ReactFlow's custom node API takes a raw React component, not a shadcn component, so these are built from scratch using CSS Modules and inline SVG for performance. (2) The TokenStreamDisplay component inside AgentNode — needs precise control over DOM updates and the RAF flush cycle. (3) The TraceTimeline on the run detail page — a custom SVG Gantt chart; no off-the-shelf component handles the variable-width parallel-branch layout needed. (4) The canvas toolbar and minimap wrappers — ReactFlow provides minimap and controls as React components that accept className only; styled via Tailwind CSS variables. (5) The SSE-driven CostCounter — a custom hook + display component with requestAnimationFrame batching. The SystemPromptEditor uses CodeMirror 6 (not shadcn) for syntax highlighting, variable insertion ({{}}-style template vars), and line-count display. The ModelSelector is a custom Combobox built on Radix Popover + Radix Command (the same primitives shadcn's Combobox uses) with virtual scrolling via @tanstack/virtual for providers with 50+ model variants. TanStack Router is used for routing (typed routes, file-based, loader pattern). TanStack Query handles all server state (workflow list, agent list, run history, provider status) with stale-while-revalidate caching. Zustand handles all client-only state as described in stateManagement. Framer Motion is used sparingly: only for the HumanGateModal enter/exit animation, the WorkflowCard hover lift effect, and the OnboardingWizard step transitions. All canvas animations are CSS-only as described in realtimeUIDesign.animationApproach.
criticalImplementationDetails: ReactFlow must be initialized with nodeTypes and edgeTypes defined as STABLE object references (useMemo or module-level const) — defining them inline in the JSX causes ReactFlow to remount all nodes on every parent render, which is catastrophic for performance during SSE streaming.

The canvasStore separation from runStore is the single most important architectural decision for streaming performance. If nodeStatuses were inside the same store as nodes/edges, every token event would trigger React reconciliation across all ReactFlow node instances. With separate stores and per-node selectors, only the streaming node re-renders during token delivery.

API keys MUST be stored encrypted on the backend (AES-256-GCM with a server-side key from environment variables), not in localStorage or sessionStorage. The frontend sends keys to POST /api/providers/{id}/key, receives a masked display string back, and never stores the raw key client-side. The backend uses keys server-side when proxying LLM API calls — the frontend never calls LLM APIs directly, preventing key exposure in browser network tab.

The workflow JSON schema must version itself (schema_version field). Introduce a WorkflowMigrator utility (a series of pure transform functions keyed by version) that upgrades older JSONs on import. This is critical because users will export workflows today and re-import them after breaking schema changes.

HumanGateNode must be idempotent on reconnect. If the SSE connection drops while a gate is pending, the reconnect resync (GET /api/runs/{runId}/state) must return the pending gate in its response, allowing the HumanGateOverlay to re-render without losing context. The backend must NOT re-send the gate event on SSE reconnect — only the state endpoint provides it.

ReactFlow's onNodesChange/onEdgesChange fire on every drag move event (60fps). The undo history debounce (300ms) is mandatory — without it, a single node drag would push 60+ snapshots per second into the history stack, exhausting memory in seconds.

The ToolNode endpoint auth credentials (API keys, bearer tokens) must be stored as named secrets in providerStore (under a 'tool_secrets' namespace) and referenced in the workflow JSON by secret ID, never by value. When the workflow JSON is exported, secret values are omitted and replaced with placeholder strings. The importer must prompt the user to re-enter secrets for any placeholders found.

For the cost counter, token pricing must be stored client-side as a static JSON table (model_id -> input_cost_per_1k, output_cost_per_1k) that is fetched from GET /api/models/pricing on app startup and cached in TanStack Query with a 24-hour staleTime. Cost is computed client-side from the token counts in SSE events, not sent as dollar amounts over SSE — this prevents the backend from needing to maintain pricing tables in the hot path.

ReactFlow node selection state (which nodes are selected for multi-select, copy, delete) must be handled by ReactFlow's internal selection model, NOT by any external zustand store. Mirroring selection state into zustand doubles the event processing and causes subtle desyncs. Read selection state from useReactFlow().getNodes().filter(n => n.selected) when needed for batch operations.

The FanOutNode's parallel branch animation requires that each outgoing edge be identified by its branch index (stored in edge.data.branchIndex). The custom edge component uses branchIndex to stagger the CSS animation-delay by (branchIndex * 120ms), creating a visually distinct parallel flow effect without any JS timing logic.

firstTimeUser: Landing on empty dashboard — greeted by a single focused CTA: 'Connect your first API key'. No feature tour, no modal. The canvas is visible but dimmed in the background to preview what's coming.

API Key screen: user picks a provider from a visual grid (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, Groq, local/Ollama). One input field, one 'Verify & Save' button. Validation happens inline with a green checkmark or red inline error. No save-then-navigate; the key is verified immediately.

First aha moment #1 — After key verification, the model list populates in real time. User sees exact model IDs, context windows, and pricing per token. Feeling: 'This tool actually knows what it's talking about.'

Prompted to create their first agent. A drawer slides in from the right. User types a name, selects a model from the now-populated list, and writes a system prompt. A 'Preview' button at the bottom opens a lightweight inline chat to test the agent before saving.
First aha moment #2 — The inline preview actually calls the API in real time. The user sends a test message and sees streaming tokens appear. Feeling: 'My agent is already alive.'

Redirected to the canvas with their first agent node already placed in the center. A tooltip overlay (not a modal) points at the node palette on the left: 'Drag more agents here, or connect them to build a pipeline.'
User drags a second agent node onto the canvas. An animated connector hint appears between the two nodes — a faint dashed arc with a '+' midpoint indicator, suggesting they can draw a connection.

User draws a connection. A small badge on the edge shows the data passing format (text, JSON, file). First aha moment #3: the canvas automatically validates the connection and shows a green edge if compatible, gray if the edge needs a transformer.

User clicks 'Run'. A slim progress bar appears at the top of the canvas. Nodes light up one by one with a pulsing ring. Streaming tokens from the currently running node appear inside the node face itself.

Run completes. The Run History panel slides in from the bottom with the cost, duration, and per-node token counts. The user clicks a node in history to replay just that step. Feeling: 'I can debug individual steps.'

Empty state for a brand-new account: the dashboard shows three starter templates ('Summarize & Translate', 'Research Pipeline', 'Code Review Chain') as clickable preview cards. Clicking one loads the template onto the canvas with placeholder agents ready to configure.
powerUser: Opens the app. Keyboard shortcut Cmd+K opens the command palette. Types workflow name, hits Enter — canvas opens directly.
Uses keyboard shortcut N to add a new agent node at the last cursor position. Fills in model and prompt using Tab-navigation inside the config panel without touching the mouse.
Duplicates a complex node with Cmd+D, drags the copy to a parallel branch, changes one parameter. Both branches are connected to a merge node.
Uses variable bindings: types {{node.output}} inside a system prompt. Autocomplete suggests all upstream node output keys. No manual wiring needed for data passing.
Triggers a run with Cmd+Enter. Watches the log panel (keyboard shortcut L to toggle). Filters logs by node name using the search bar.
A node fails. The failed node pulses red. The power user clicks it to expand the error inline. They see the raw API response, the prompt that was sent, and a one-click 'Retry from here' button.
Opens the Run History table (keyboard shortcut H). Sorts by cost descending. Identifies that one workflow is consuming 80% of spend. Drills in with a per-node cost waterfall chart.
Exports a workflow as a JSON definition. Pastes it into a colleague's account via Import on the canvas toolbar.
Sets up a webhook trigger node on the canvas so the workflow fires automatically from an external system.
Uses the agents library panel (keyboard shortcut A) to reuse an agent definition across three different workflows without duplicating configuration.
ahaMoments: Real-time token streaming inside the node face during a run — the canvas becomes a live theater.
Inline agent preview chat before saving — confirms the prompt works before wiring it into a workflow.
Variable autocomplete ({{node.output}}) in system prompt fields — data plumbing feels like writing code, not clicking.
Per-node cost breakdown in run history — users immediately understand where money goes.
One-click 'Retry from here' on a failed node — debugging feels surgical, not destructive.
Template gallery as the first screen for new users — the product shows its own capability before the user builds anything.

keyContent: Recent Workflows grid (last 6, with thumbnail previews of the canvas)
Recent Runs table (workflow name, status badge, duration, cost, timestamp)
Spend summary widget (this month vs last month, bar chart)
Quick-action buttons: New Workflow, Import Workflow

Table view: name, agent count, last run status, last run time, total runs, total spend
Tag/folder filtering in the left sidebar
Bulk select → Delete, Archive, Export
New Workflow button → opens blank canvas

Main canvas area: infinite scrollable, zoomable grid
Top toolbar: workflow name (editable inline), Save button, Run button, Undo/Redo, Zoom controls, Minimap toggle
Right panel (context-sensitive): Node config panel when a node is selected, Edge config panel when a connection is selected
Bottom panel (toggleable): Run log, expanded when a run is active
Run status bar: slim strip at top of canvas showing overall run state when running

keyContent: Grid of agent cards: name, provider logo, model name, last used date, workflow count
Search and filter by provider, model family

Click agent card → opens agent detail/edit view
Usage graph per agent (runs over time)

Provider and model picker

Status filter tabs: All, Running, Completed, Failed
Date range picker
Cost trend sparkline in a summary header

Connected card shows: key prefix (sk-...xxxx), verified badge, last used date, estimated spend this month
